steps.action: The term also refers to the music and the charac with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, accent beat, grounded.
steps.text: The term also refers to the music and the charac with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: Dances of this name are encountered from about 1
steps.text: Dances of this name are encountered from about 1500 and the term is used for dances still danced in France today.

steps.action: Before 1500, the only dance-related use of this
steps.text: Before 1500, the only dance-related use of this word is the "swaying" step of the basse danse.

steps.action: The branle was danced by a chain of dancers, usu
steps.text: The branle was danced by a chain of dancers, usually in couples, with linked arms or holding hands.

steps.action: The dance alternated a number of larger sideways
steps.text: The dance alternated a number of larger sideways steps to the left (often four) with the same number of smaller steps to the right so that the chain moved gradually to the left.

steps.action: A variety of branles, attributed to different re
steps.text: A variety of branles, attributed to different regions, were danced in sequence, so that the suite of branle music gives one of the earliest examples of the classical suite of dances.

steps.action: Some of these dances were reserved for specific
steps.text: Some of these dances were reserved for specific age groups - the branle de Bourgogne, for instance, for the youngest dancers.

steps.action: Among the dance's courtly relations may be the b
steps.text: Among the dance's courtly relations may be the basse danse and the passepied which latter, though it is in triple time, Rabelais and Thoinot Arbeau (1589) identify as a type of Breton branle.

steps.action: Antonius de Arena briefly describes the steps fo
steps.text: Antonius de Arena briefly describes the steps for the double and single branle, and John Marston's The Malcontent (1604) sketches the choreography of one type.

steps.action: The male dancer moves away from his partner befo
steps.text: The male dancer moves away from his partner before performing a "galliard trick of twenty"—apparently a number of capers or leaps in the manner of the galliard—before returning to the conventional ending.

steps.action: The piece is a collection of six folk dances arr
steps.text: The piece is a collection of six folk dances arranged originally for four hands piano, but was then arranged by Warlock for both string orchestra and full orchestra.

steps.text: Dance descriptions (in German), sheet music and audios.

steps.action: Such suites generally ended with a gavotte, whic
steps.text: Such suites generally ended with a gavotte, which seems then to have been regarded as a species of branle.

steps.action: Some aristocratic branles included pantomime ele
steps.text: Some aristocratic branles included pantomime elements, such the branle de Poitou, the possible ancestor of the minuet, which acts out gestures of courtship.

steps.action: Branle music is generally in common time somewha
steps.text: Branle music is generally in common time somewhat like the gavotte, though some variants, like that of Poitou, are in triple time.

steps.action: Branles were danced walking, running, gliding, o
steps.text: Branles were danced walking, running, gliding, or skipping depending on the speed of the music.

steps.action: According to Arbeau, every ball began with the s
steps.text: According to Arbeau, every ball began with the same four branles: the double, the single, the gay and the Burgundian branle.

steps.action: The double branle had a simple form involving tw
steps.text: The double branle had a simple form involving two phrases of two bars each.

steps.action: On the other hand, Arbeau identifies some branle
steps.text: On the other hand, Arbeau identifies some branles as adapted to ballet and mime.
